page: Amazon Web Services g4dn.4xlarge is a GPU-accelerated server instance powered by the Nitro hypervisor. It features an Intel Xeon 8259CL x86_64 processor with 16 vCPUs (8 physical cores) running at 2.5 GHz, supported by 64.0 GB of DDR4 memory. For acceleration, the instance includes a single NVIDIA Turing T4 GPU with 16 GB of VRAM. Local storage is provided via a 225 GB NVMe SSD, and the instance offers a baseline network bandwidth of 20 Gbps.

Performance benchmarks indicate a clear division between CPU and GPU capabilities. Single-core CPU performance is weak, falling into the bottom 25% for stress-ng and compression tasks. However, multi-core CPU performance, memory bandwidth, and general database operations score in the average range. The standout capability is GPU-driven machine learning; LLM inference benchmarks for small (135M) and medium (7B) models rank in the top 10% and top 25% respectively, demonstrating strong efficiency for prompt processing and text generation.

From a cost-efficiency perspective, the g4dn.4xlarge offers a balanced entry point for GPU-accelerated workloads without the high expense of top-tier accelerator instances. The inclusion of local NVMe storage and optimized network performance offsets the lower single-core CPU speeds. This instance is best suited for machine learning inference, small-to-medium LLM deployment, graphics-intensive applications, and video transcoding.
description: Amazon Web Services g4dn.4xlarge is a GPU-accelerated instance featuring 16 vCPUs from an Intel Xeon 8259CL processor, 64.0 GB of DDR4 memory, and a single NVIDIA Turing T4 GPU with 16 GB of VRAM. It includes 225 GB of local NVMe SSD storage and a 20 Gbps baseline network bandwidth. While single-core CPU benchmarks are weak, multi-core performance and memory bandwidth remain average. The instance excels in machine learning, with LLM inference speeds for small and medium models placing in the top 10% and top 25% respectively. This hardware configuration offers a cost-effective profile for GPU-dependent workloads, making it highly suitable for machine learning inference, graphics rendering, and video transcoding.
